overview_purpose_1.description: Explains Craft's purpose as a small cross-platform Minecraft-like game, how the main client organizes world state and rendering, and how configuration, persistence, multiplayer, and build setup fit together.

setup_build_run_1.description: Explains how Craft is built from source, how the client and multiplayer server are launched, and how the Python server depends on the compiled world-generation library.

gameplay_controls_commands_1.description: Explains how Craft turns player input into movement, view changes, block editing, signs, chat, slash commands, hit testing, and collision behavior.

world_generation_chunks_1.description: Explains how Craft generates deterministic terrain, stores block data by chunk, loads and updates chunks around the player, and builds renderable chunk buffers.

rendering_graphics_pipeline_1.description: Explains how Craft builds chunk, block, plant, sign, sky, text, and player geometry into OpenGL buffers, then renders them with shaders, culling, daylight, fog, transparency, and ambient occlusion.

persistence_database_cache_1.description: Explains how Craft persists world edits, player state, signs, lights, auth tokens, and multiplayer cache keys through sqlite and a background write queue.

multiplayer_protocol_server_1.description: Explains Craft's socket-based multiplayer protocol, how the C client enters online mode, how chunk cache keys and world updates flow through the Python server, and how login, chat, players, time, lights, and signs are synchronized.
